EP.20 Learning JS From Zero — Day 20: Getting to Know the BOM
JavaScript
RE：從零開始的學習 JS 生活
第 11 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

Day 20 of my reincarnated JavaScript journey. This time we're looking at the BOM (Browser Object Model) — what it is, how it differs from the DOM, and the most common BOM operations you'll actually reach for, like history, location, screen, navigator, and print.
/en/javascript/2019/10/04/js-reincarnation-19-this/
EP.19 Learning JS From Zero — Day 19: Making Sense of this

Day 19 of my reincarnation, and this time we're tackling this — one of the most common keywords in JavaScript, and also one of the easiest to get burned by. This post focuses on what this actually is, why it points where it points, and how strict mode changes the picture.
/en/javascript/2019/10/03/js-from-zero-18-localstorage/
JS From Zero (Day 18): localStorage — Save Data Right in the Browser

Day eighteen of the reincarnation. This time we look at another way to store data in the browser — localStorage. We'll cover what it is, how it differs from Cookie and sessionStorage, and how to set, read, delete, clear, and update values, plus a quick real-world example.
/en/php/2019/10/02/thinkphp-day-31-the-end-is-just-another-beginning/
Day 31 - Learning ThinkPHP From Scratch: The End Is Just Another Beginning
PHP
MySQL
ThinkPHP 5.1
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch
11th iThome Ironman Contest

I picked Day 31 as the wrap-up for this series, so this post is mostly my closing thoughts on finishing the Ironman contest. I talk about why I was terrified of not making it to the finish line, why I chose ThinkPHP over Laravel or Yii, and I round things off with links to my other series so you've got somewhere to go next.
/en/javascript/2019/10/02/js-from-zero-ep17-cookie/
JS From Zero EP.17: How Cookies Actually Work in the Browser

Day seventeen of starting JavaScript from scratch. This post is all about cookies — what they are, the difference between memory cookies and disk cookies, how they pair up with sessions for login, and how to read and write them in the browser with document.cookie.
/en/javascript/2019/10/01/js-from-zero-16-operator-precedence-associativity/
Why Is 3 < 2 < 1 True in JavaScript? Operator Precedence and Associativity

Day 16 of the reincarnation. This post covers operator precedence and associativity in JavaScript. We'll look at why operators have a priority order, and the classic gotcha where 3 < 2 < 1 somehow evaluates to true. Once you understand associativity and type coercion, that weird result starts to make total sense.
/en/php/2019/10/01/thinkphp-day-30-deploy-to-heroku/
Day 30 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Deploying to Heroku

Time to ship it. In this post I'll walk through deploying the ThinkPHP system we've been building over the last few chapters onto Heroku. We'll create a Heroku app, fix the inevitable 403, set up a ClearDB MySQL database, run our migrations and seeds, and patch a handful of deployment gotchas (missing CSS/JS, mb_substr, the captcha, and timestamps).

Getting Bombarded with Questions at the HexSchool Alumni Meetup

On 2019/09/29 (Teacher's Day), HexSchool held an alumni meetup. There was free chat time during the event, so this post records the process of me getting bombarded with student questions (?!).
/en/php/2019/09/30/think-php-day-29-cli-commands-and-migrations/
ThinkPHP CLI Commands and Database Migrations (Day 29)

Next up we'll get to know some of ThinkPHP's commonly used basic CLI commands. But the main focus of this post is migrations, because they'll affect how we work in the later chapters. We'll walk through the everyday commands, install think-migration, create migration files for our tables, write up() and down(), and finally seed the database with default data.
/en/javascript/2019/09/30/re-learning-js-15-vanilla-ajax/
RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 15: Vanilla AJAX (XMLHttpRequest)

Day 15 of my reincarnated JS life. This time we dig into AJAX — what it actually is, why it changed the web, and how the native API XMLHttpRequest works under the hood. We'll also peek at the modern Fetch API and the old jQuery AJAX style so you can compare them side by side.
